title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 5 (Fragment 6)
text:
“Oh, why?” complained Lucia, Paolo and young Cousin Lena.

“I know,” said Aunt Maria. “Like school again. Start writing.”

“It’s exploiting children, that’s what we’re doing,” Rosa said cheerfully. “There are probably laws against it, so do complain.”

“Don’t worry, I will,” said Lucia. “I am doing.”

“As long as you write while you grumble,” said Rosa.

“It’s a new spell-scrip for the Army,” Uncle Lorenzo explained. “It’s very urgent.”

“It’s hard. It’s all new words,” Paolo grumbled.

“Your father made it last night,” said Aunt Maria. “Get writing. We’ll be watching for mistakes.”

When finally, stiff-necked and with red splodges on their fingers, they were let out into the yard, Tonino discovered that he had barely time to unwrap the parcel before supper. Supper was early that night, so that the elder Montanas could put in another shift on the army-spells before bedtime.

“It’s worse than working on the Old Bridge,” said Lucia. “What’s that, Tonino? Who sent it?”

The parcel was promisingly book-shaped. It bore the stamp and the arms of the University of Caprona. This was the only indication Tonino had that Uncle Umberto had sent it, for, when he wrenched off the thick brown paper, there was no letter, not even a card. There was only a new shiny book. Tonino’s face beamed. At least Uncle Umberto knew this much about him. He turned the book lovingly over. It was called The Boy Who Saved His Country , and the cover was the same shiny, pimpled red leather as the great volumes of war-spells.

“Is Uncle Umberto trying to give you a hint, or something?” Paolo asked, amused. He and Lucia and Corinna leaned over Tonino while he flipped through the pages. There were pictures, to Tonino’s delight. Soldiers rode horses, soldiers rode machines; a boy hung from a rope and scrambled up the frowning wall of a fortress; and, most exciting of all, a boy stood on a rock with a flag, confronting a whole troop of ferocious-looking dragoons. Sighing with anticipation, Tonino turned to Chapter One: How Giorgio uncovered an Enemy Plot .

“Supper!” howled Aunt Gina from the yard. “Oh I shall go mad! Nobody attends to me!”

Tonino was forced to shut the lovely book again and hurry down to the dining room. He watched Aunt Gina anxiously as she doled out minestrone. She looked so hectic that he was convinced Benvenuto must have been at work in the kitchen again.

“It’s all right,” Rosa said. “It’s just she thought she’d got a line from the Angel of Caprona . Then the soup boiled over and she forgot it again.”

Aunt Gina was distinctly tearful. “With so much to do, my memory is like a sieve,” she kept saying. “Now I’ve let you all down.”

“Of course you haven’t, Gina my dear,” said Old Niccolo. “This is nothing to worry about. It will come back to you.”

“But I can’t even remember what language it was in!” wailed Aunt Gina.

Everyone tried to console her. They sprinkled grated cheese on their soup and slurped it with special relish, to show Aunt Gina how much they appreciated her, but Aunt Gina continued to sniff and accuse herself. Then Rinaldo thought of pointing out that she had got further than anyone else in the Casa Montana. “None of the rest of us has any of the Angel of Caprona to forget,” he said, giving Aunt Gina his best smile.

“Bah!” said Aunt Gina. “Turning on the charm, Rinaldo Montana!” But she seemed a good deal more cheerful after that.

Tonino was glad Benvenuto had nothing to do with it this time. He looked around for Benvenuto. Benvenuto usually took up a good position for stealing scraps, near the serving table. But tonight he was nowhere to be seen. Nor, for that matter, was Marco.

vocabulary.definition: to blame someone
vocabulary.example: ...but Aunt Gina continued to sniff and accuse herself.

vocabulary.definition: finding something funny or entertaining
vocabulary.example: “Is Uncle Umberto trying to give you a hint, or something?” Paolo asked, amused.

vocabulary.definition: the act of looking forward to something with excitement
vocabulary.example: Sighing with anticipation, Tonino turned to Chapter One: How Giorgio uncovered an Enemy Plot.

vocabulary.definition: qualities that attract or delight others
vocabulary.example: “Bah!” said Aunt Gina. “Turning on the charm, Rinaldo Montana!”

vocabulary.definition: in a happy and positive way
vocabulary.example: “It’s exploiting children, that’s what we’re doing,” Rosa said cheerfully.

vocabulary.definition: express dissatisfaction or annoyance
vocabulary.example: There are probably laws against it, so do complain.

vocabulary.definition: express dissatisfaction or annoyance about something
vocabulary.example: “Oh, why?” complained Lucia, Paolo and young Cousin Lena.

vocabulary.definition: to face someone or something, often in a challenging way
vocabulary.example: ...a boy stood on a rock with a flag, confronting a whole troop of ferocious-looking dragoons.

vocabulary.definition: making use of a situation or person unfairly for one's own advantage
vocabulary.example: “It’s exploiting children, that’s what we’re doing,” Rosa said cheerfully.

vocabulary.definition: savage and fierce
vocabulary.example: ...a boy stood on a rock with a flag, confronting a whole troop of ferocious-looking dragoons.

vocabulary.definition: a military stronghold or fortified building
vocabulary.example: ...scrambled up the frowning wall of a fortress;

vocabulary.definition: showing displeasure or worry by drawing eyebrows together
vocabulary.example: ...a boy hung from a rope and scrambled up the frowning wall of a fortress...

vocabulary.definition: to complain in a quiet but persistent way
vocabulary.example: “As long as you write while you grumble,” said Rosa.

vocabulary.definition: complained quietly and angrily
vocabulary.example: “It’s hard. It’s all new words,” Paolo grumbled.

vocabulary.definition: to shout loudly, often in frustration or excitement
vocabulary.example: “Supper!” howled Aunt Gina from the yard.

vocabulary.definition: a sign or piece of information that suggests something
vocabulary.example: This was the only indication Tonino had that Uncle Umberto had sent it...

vocabulary.definition: in a way that shows affection or care
vocabulary.example: He turned the book lovingly over.

vocabulary.definition: a package or wrapped bundle
vocabulary.example: Tonino discovered that he had barely time to unwrap the parcel before supper.

vocabulary.definition: covered with small raised spots
vocabulary.example: It was called The Boy Who Saved His Country, and the cover was the same shiny, pimpled red leather...

vocabulary.definition: in a way that suggests future success
vocabulary.example: The parcel was promisingly book-shaped.

vocabulary.definition: enjoyed greatly
vocabulary.example: They sprinkled grated cheese on their soup and slurped it with special relish...

vocabulary.definition: a period of work time
vocabulary.example: Supper was early that night, so that the elder Montanas could put in another shift on the army-spells before bedtime.

vocabulary.definition: a utensil used to strain solids from liquids; metaphorically someone who forgets things easily
vocabulary.example: “With so much to do, my memory is like a sieve,” she kept saying.

vocabulary.definition: irregularly shaped marks or spots
vocabulary.example: When finally, stiff-necked and with red splodges on their fingers, they were let out into the yard...

vocabulary.definition: very stubborn; unwilling to change opinions or attitudes
vocabulary.example: When finally, stiff-necked and with red splodges on their fingers, they were let out into the yard...

vocabulary.definition: an evening meal
vocabulary.example: Supper was early that night, so that the elder Montanas could put in another shift...

vocabulary.definition: showing or causing tears
vocabulary.example: Aunt Gina was distinctly tearful.

vocabulary.definition: requiring immediate attention
vocabulary.example: “It’s a new spell-scrip for the Army,” Uncle Lorenzo explained. “It’s very urgent.”

vocabulary.definition: collections of written or printed sheets bound together as books
vocabulary.example: ...as the great volumes of war-spells.

vocabulary.definition: pulled or twisted suddenly and violently
vocabulary.example: ...for, when he wrenched off the thick brown paper, there was no letter...
